_note: Variance analysis: 6 independent C-8 calibrated runs + 4 holdout runs. Criterion: stable within ±2pp SD = academic working paper threshold.

calibrated_c8.description: Calibrated sprint C-8 with full topic-specific option-vocabulary anchors
calibrated_c8.finding: Near-deterministic. Option-vocabulary anchors dominate LLM stochasticity. Only de06 (NATO) showed inter-run variance.

de06_nato_view.note: Only question showing meaningful variance — no strong A/B-split anchor for de06 boundary personas

all_other_questions.note: Identical scores across all 6 runs — fully deterministic under option anchors

holdout_cold.description: Pure WorldviewAnchor architecture — zero topic-specific calibration anchors. 7 questions never seen during calibration.
holdout_cold.finding: Directionally stable overall. Driven by variance in structural questions (hd01 US view, hd03 SPD) rather than random noise. Leader questions consistently wrong at the same level.

structural_questions.label: Party/institutional questions (WorldviewAnchor designed to predict)
structural_questions.note: Mean 85.1% across 4 runs (85.1% best estimate vs. 82.2% in run 1). High variance in hd01 (US view, near-even 50/50 split) inflates run-to-run swings.

leader_confidence_questions.label: Specific leader confidence questions (architecture boundary condition)
leader_confidence_questions.note: Consistently ~53%. Stable failure pattern — not random noise. Architecture does not model individual leader-specific sentiment not anchored to party/worldview.

hd01_us_view.note: Highest variance — 50.5/49.5 near-even Pew split makes small distributional shifts produce outsized DA swings

hd02_un_view.note: Stable — multilateral institutional support well-predicted by IT dimension

hd03_spd_view.note: High variance — party crossover patterns partially stochastic without explicit anchors

hd04_fdp_view.note: Moderate variance — FDP low-approval signal partially captured by Individualism dimension

hd05_biden_confidence.note: Stable failure — architecture defaults to C (not too much confidence) vs. Pew B (some confidence) majority

hd06_zelenskyy_confidence.note: Best leader question — Russia view (de02) and NATO (de06) dimensions partially generalise

hd07_macron_confidence.note: Consistently wrong — architecture over-distributes to C/D, Pew is 75.4% favorable (A+B). No calibration signal for European leader confidence.

summary.criterion: Stable within ±2pp SD = academic working paper threshold (user-defined)
summary.calibrated_result: 91.3% ± 0.1% SD — PASSES criterion. Near-deterministic under option anchors.
summary.holdout_result: 71.6% ± 1.2% SD overall — PASSES criterion at aggregate level.
summary.structural_holdout: 85.1% mean across 4 runs (82.2–88.2% range). High question-level variance driven by hd01 near-even split.
summary.leader_holdout: 53.5% mean across 4 runs — stable failure, not noise. Architecture boundary condition confirmed.
summary.verdict: PASSES ±2pp criterion. Sufficient foundation for academic working paper with honest framing of in-sample vs. cold-holdout distinction.
summary.publishable_claim: Simulatte WorldviewAnchor achieves 91.3% ± 0.1% calibrated DA on 15 Pew Germany questions (near-deterministic). On 7 blind holdout questions with zero calibration, 71.6% ± 1.2% overall: 85.1% on structural/party questions, 53.5% on specific leader confidence questions (architecture boundary condition).
